answer:
**In Allama Iqbal's poem "Mehr-e-Roshan" (ch. 32 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*), the bright sun—titled in the work itself—is depicted as setting at dusk, prompting a cosmic mourning.** The opening verses portray it vividly:

> The shining sun has gone from sight, the veil is lifted from the face of night,[^1]  
> For whom does it prepare this dark array, this robe of grey?  
> All Nature’s court is mourning for the sun, now passed away.[^2]

This sets a contemplative tone, framing the sunset as a veil lifting from night's face while nature grieves the sun's departure, amid the speaker's inquiry to the dead about the afterlife. The sun symbolizes transience, contrasting with existential questions that dominate the poem.[^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 32 "Mehr-e-roshan" (translation), [p-202a06](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/32-mehr-e-roshan/translation/#p-202a06).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 32 "Mehr-e-roshan" (translation), [p-9e70bf](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/32-mehr-e-roshan/translation/#p-9e70bf).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 32 "Mehr-e-roshan" (translation), [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/32-mehr-e-roshan/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/32-mehr-e-roshan/translation/).
